Simple Psych

← Back to all diagnoses

Anxiety Disorders

Panic Disorder

ICD-10-CM: F41.0

Advertisement
Google Ads Banner Space

1. Criteria

DSM Criteria

Criterion A

  • Recurrent unexpected panic attacks occur.
  • A panic attack is an abrupt surge of intense fear or intense discomfort that reaches a peak within minutes.
  • During the surge, 4 or more symptoms occur such as palpitations, sweating, trembling, shortness of breath, feelings of choking, chest discomfort, nausea, dizziness, chills or heat sensations, paresthesias, derealization or depersonalization, fear of losing control, or fear of dying.

DSM Criteria

Criterion B

  • At least one attack has been followed by 1 month or more of persistent concern or worry about additional attacks or their consequences.
  • At least one attack has been followed by significant maladaptive behavior change related to the attacks, such as avoidance.

DSM Criteria

Criterion C

  • The disturbance is not attributable to a substance or another medical condition.

DSM Criteria

Criterion D

  • The disturbance is not better explained by another mental disorder such as social anxiety disorder, specific phobia, OCD, PTSD, or separation anxiety disorder.

2. Context

  • Panic disorder is defined by recurrent unexpected panic attacks followed by persistent concern about additional attacks or maladaptive behavior change related to them.
  • Unexpected attacks: The attacks are not always cued by the same trigger and arrive abruptly.
  • Aftermath matters: The diagnosis requires more than attacks alone; ongoing worry or avoidance after attacks is essential.
  • Medical rule-outs: Cardiopulmonary, endocrine, vestibular, and substance-related causes should be reviewed carefully.
  • Clinical focus: Most common clinician-rated and self-report tool for panic disorder severity.

3. Validated scales

Panic Disorder Severity Scale

Most common clinician-rated and self-report tool for panic disorder severity.

CGI-S

Fast global severity impression during follow-up.

4. FDA approved treatments

FDA-indicated medications commonly used for panic disorder

Several SSRIs carry panic-disorder labeling; verify exact formulation and patient age indications in the current package insert.

  • Sertraline
  • Paroxetine
  • Fluoxetine

Common off-label medications

  • Clonazepam
  • Escitalopram
  • Venlafaxine XR

Interventional psychiatry modalities

  • TMS is being explored for panic-spectrum symptoms.
  • ECT is not standard for primary panic disorder.
  • Ketamine remains investigational rather than routine care here.

  2. CBT and medication for panic disorder Comparative treatment literature
